remedies.remedy_desc: Peppermint tea helps with cough because it contains menthol, a substance with natural decongestant and expectorant properties that help to thin mucus and soothe inflamed throats, making it easier to expel phlegm and reduce the intensity and frequency of coughing fits. The soothing warmth of the tea also helps to alleviate irritation in the throat, providing relief from irritants that can trigger coughing. Furthermore, peppermint tea has anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects that can help to reduce inflammation and pain in the respiratory tract, reducing the severity of cough and other cold and flu symptoms. Additionally, peppermint tea is caffeine-free, making it a gentle and relaxing way to hydrate and support the immune system, which is crucial for fighting off infections and promoting recovery. Finally, peppermint tea has been used for centuries in traditional medicine as a natural remedy for cough, cold, and respiratory ailments, and its efficacy has been supported by some scientific studies, making it a popular and trusted choice for those seeking a natural way to manage cough and other symptoms of respiratory illness.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rich in menthol and rosmarinic acid, peppermint leaves help soothe throat irritation and coughs by providing a cooling sensation and anti-inflammatory effects.
Flowers: Packed with essential oils, peppermint flowers offer calming properties for a cough-ridden throat and promote expectoration of mucus.
Stems: Containing similar compounds to leaves, peppermint stems contribute to a soothing tea that aids in relieving respiratory ailments, including persistent coughs.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Acts as an expectorant, helping to thin and expel mucus, thus providing relief from cough.
Mentone: Possesses anti-tussive properties, reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing.
Menthyl acetate: Has calming and soothing effects, helping to alleviate irritation in the throat and suppress cough.
Rosmarinic acid: Exhibits anti-inflammatory and anti-oxidant properties, aiding in reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract and improving respiratory symptoms, including cough.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, boil 2 cups of water for 5 minutes.
Add 1 heaped teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves to the boiling water.
Let the peppermint leaves steep in the hot water for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea into a mug, discarding the spent peppermint leaves.
Slowly sip the tea while it's still warm, up to three times a day as needed.

remedies.remedy_desc: Licorice tea helps with cough because it contains a compound called glycyrrhizin, which helps to soothe inflammation and irritation in the throat. This compound also helps to loosen and expel mucus, making it easier to expel phlegm. Additionally, licorice tea is known to have expectorant properties, which can help reduce the severity and frequency of coughing fits. Furthermore, the soothing and calming effects of licorice tea can help reduce stress and promote better sleep, both of which can be beneficial for those suffering from a cough. However, it's important to note that licorice tea should not be consumed in large quantities or for extended periods of time, as it can have adverse effects on blood pressure and other health conditions.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the licorice plant contain glycyrrhizin, which helps soothe sore throats and reduce coughing.
Stems: The stems of the licorice plant contain flavonoids that have anti-inflammatory properties, helping to alleviate symptoms of respiratory infections.

Leaves: The leaves of the licorice plant contain various beneficial compounds, including flavonoids and volatile oils, which have expectorant and soothing properties, making them useful in treating coughs.
Flowers: The flowers of the licorice plant contain antioxidants that help boost the immune system and reduce inflammation, providing relief for coughs and other respiratory issues.
remedy_constituents: Glycyrrhizin: This compound is the primary active component in licorice and has been shown to have anti-tussive (cough-suppressing) properties.
Liquiritigenin: This flavonoid found in licorice has been found to have antitussive and expectorant effects, helping to alleviate cough and expel mucus.
Liquiritin: This flavonoid has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antitussive effects, helping to reduce cough and soothe irritated throat tissues.
Isoliquiritigenin: This flavonoid has been found to have antitussive, expectorant, and anti-inflammatory effects, making it helpful in treating cough and related symptoms.
Glabridin: This isoflavonoid has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antitussive effects, helping to reduce cough and soothe irritated airways.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, boil 1 cup of water for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 tablespoon of crushed licorice root to the boiling water.
Add honey or lemon to taste, if desired, and drink while warm.

remedies.remedy_desc: Slippery elm tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel can coat and soothe the mouth, throat, stomach, and intestines, which helps to reduce irritation and inflammation. As a result, slippery elm tea can help to relieve coughing caused by irritation in the throat, such as from a common cold or flu. Additionally, the gel can help to remove excess mucus from the respiratory system, making it easier to cough up and expel. Slippery elm tea has also been shown to have anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting properties, which can help the body to fight off infections and reduce inflammation in the respiratory system. Overall, slippery elm tea can provide relief for a cough by soothing and protecting the throat, reducing inflammation, removing excess mucus, and supporting the immune system.
remedy_parts: Bark: The bark of the slippery elm plant is commonly used to make medicinal tea for cough due to its mucilage content, which can soothe and protect irritated mucous membranes.
Leaves: Slippery elm leaves are used for medicinal tea for cough because they contain beneficial compounds that can help reduce inflammation and relieve irritation in the respiratory tract.
Buds: The buds of the slippery elm plant are used in medicinal tea for cough as they are rich in antioxidants and have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate coughing and sore throat.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: Slippery elm contains a significant amount of mucilage, which when coming into contact with water, forms a slippery, gel-like substance. This substance coats and soothes irritated membranes, providing relief from coughing.
Tannins: Slippery elm contains tannins, which have astringent properties. These astringents help to tighten and tone the mucous membranes, reducing inflammation, and easing coughing.
Starch: Slippery elm contains starch, which can help to soothe and calm the digestive tract, reducing irritation that may contribute to a persistent cough.
Ascorbic acid: Slippery elm contains small amounts of ascorbic acid, which is known to boost the immune system and help the body fight off infections, including those that cause coughs.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of slippery elm bark.
Bring the mixture to a boil, then reduce heat and let it simmer for 5-10 minutes.
Remove from heat, add honey to taste (optional), and let it cool down slightly.
Sip the tea slowly, allowing it to coat your throat and soothe your cough.

remedies.remedy_desc: Marshmallow tea helps with cough because it contains a substance called mucilage, which becomes a slick gel when mixed with water. This gel can coat and soothe irritated mucous membranes found in the mouth, throat, and lungs, providing relief from constant coughing. Moreover, the gel also creates a barrier that can protect these areas from further irritation and inflammation, reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Additionally, the anti-inflammatory properties of marshmallow tea can further alleviate irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, while its expectorant effects can help loosen and expel mucus, making it easier to expel and clear the airways. Lastly, marshmallow tea's demulcent and soothing properties can promote rest and relaxation, which is essential for a good night's sleep, particularly for individuals who experience coughing spells that worsen at night.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage, which can help soothe a cough by coating the throat and reducing irritation.
Flowers: Marshmallow flowers also contain mucilage, which can help relieve symptoms of a dry cough and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Roots: The roots of the marshmallow plant are rich in mucilage and starch, making them a popular choice for treating coughs and sore throats, as they can help soothe and protect the mucous membranes.

Bark: The bark of the marshmallow plant contains a high concentration of mucilage, which can help alleviate symptoms of a chesty cough by expectorating mucus and reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract.

Stems: The stems of the marshmallow plant contain mucilage and other compounds that have been shown to have anti-tussive (cough-suppressing) properties, making them useful for treating coughs and respiratory ailments.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: The mucilage in marshmallow contains polysaccharides that form a slick gel, which coats and soothes irritated mucous membranes, providing relief from coughs and sore throats.
Ascorbic Acid: Marshmallow contains small amounts of vitamin C, which supports the immune system and can help the body fight infections that may cause coughs.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids found in marshmallow possess anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviate cough symptoms.
Pectin: Pectin is a type of soluble fiber present in marshmallow, which helps soothe irritated membranes and can help relieve cough by adding a protective layer in the throat.

Polysaccharides: These complex carbohydrates in marshmallow form a soothing gel when combined with water, which can help reduce inflammation and irritation in the respiratory tract, offering relief from coughs.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water and add 1-2 teaspoons of dried marshmallow root; let it simmer for 10-15 minutes.
Remove from heat, then add 1-2 marshmallows or 1 teaspoon of marshmallow root extract; cover and let it steep for 5-10 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove solids, then add honey or sugar to taste.
Optional: add a pinch of cinnamon or a slice of lemon for added flavor and potential cough-relieving properties.
Consume the tea while warm, up to three times a day, as needed to soothe a persistent cough.

remedies.remedy_desc: Thyme tea helps with cough because it contains compounds with antispasmodic and expectorant properties, such as flavonoids and volatile oils, particularly thymol and carvacrol. These components help soothe the throat, reduce inflammation, and relax the muscles in the trachea, making it easier to expel mucus and alleviating irritating coughs. Furthermore, thyme has been shown to have antimicrobial effects, which may help combat respiratory infections that can cause coughing. Additionally, thyme tea can increase the production of saliva and mucus, further lubricating and coating the throat, providing relief from constant tickling or dry coughs. Lastly, thyme's soothing and calming qualities may help promote restful sleep, allowing the body to rest and heal while minimizing the disruption caused by persistent coughing.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Thyme leaves are rich in volatile oils and flavonoids, making them effective in alleviating cough and reducing inflammation.
Flowers: Thyme flowers contain healing compounds, including flavonoids and essential oils, that help soothe coughs and fight respiratory infections.
Stems: Although less potent than leaves and flowers, thyme stems still contain medicinal properties, making them suitable for medicinal tea to treat cough.
remedy_constituents: Thymol: Thymol has antispasmodic and expectorant properties, helping to relieve coughing and expel mucus from the respiratory tract.
Carvacrol: Carvacrol has been shown to have antitussive and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to reduce cough and soothe irritated throat tissue.
Linalool: Linalool has soothing and calming effects, which can help alleviate coughing fits and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Terpinen-4-ol: Terpinen-4-ol is known for its anti-inflammatory and mucolytic properties, helping to reduce inflammation and thin mucus, making it easier to expel from the lungs.
α-Pinene: α-Pinene has bronchodilator and expectorant properties, which can help relieve coughing by opening up the airways and promoting the expulsion of mucus.
Camphene: Camphene has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and expectorant effects, which can help alleviate cough and soothe irritated throat tissue.
remedy_recipe: In a small pot, bring 1 cup of water to a boil, which takes about 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 teaspoon of dried thyme leaves or 1 tablespoon of fresh thyme to the boiling water.
Reduce heat to low, cover the pot, and let it simmer for 5-7 minutes to allow the thyme to infuse.
Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the used thyme leaves.
Sip the warm thyme tea, ideally three times a day, for its soothing properties to help alleviate a cough.

remedies.remedy_desc: Mullein tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a gel-like substance that coats the throat and reduces irritation, helping to soothe coughs. Additionally, mullein has anti-inflammatory properties that can help decrease inflammation in the respiratory tract, further alleviating coughing. The plant's saponins, specifically verbascosides, have been shown to have expectorant effects, helping to loosen and expel mucus from the lungs. Furthermore, mullein tea's soothing warmth can help relax the muscles in the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Lastly, mullein tea has been used traditionally for respiratory health due to its demulcent and expectorant properties, supporting its effectiveness in treating coughs and other respiratory conditions.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Rich in saponins, mullein flowers help loosen phlegm and soothe throat irritation.
Leaves: Containing mucilage and saponins, mullein leaves are beneficial for reducing inflammation and easing coughs.
Stems: High in marrubiin, mullein stems have expectorant properties, aiding in eliminating mucus from the respiratory tract.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: This constituent of mullein forms a soothing film over the mucous membranes in the respiratory tract, helping to reduce irritation and coughing.
Coumarins: Certain coumarins found in mullein have expectorant properties, aiding in the expulsion of mucus and alleviating persistent coughs.
Saponins: These compounds help to break up and clear phlegm from the lungs, making it easier to expel mucus and relieving the coughing reflex.
Glycosides: Specific glycosides in mullein possess antitussive qualities, reducing the severity and frequency of coughing fits.
flavonoids: Certain flavonoids in mullein have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, helping protect lung tissue and alleviate cough-related irritation.
remedy_recipe: Heat one cup of water to boiling point (10 minutes).
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried mullein leaves to a tea infuser or mesh strainer (30 seconds).
Pour the hot water over the mullein leaves and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or mesh strainer with the spent mullein leaves (1 minute).
Drink the warm mullein tea throughout the day for cough relief (instant).

remedies.remedy_desc: Coltsfoot tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a sticky substance that coats the throat and soothes irritation, providing relief from constant coughing. Additionally, coltsfoot has expectorant properties that help loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, reducing congestion and making it easier to breathe. The tea also has anti-inflammatory effects, which can alleviate swelling in the throat and airways, further easing coughing fits. Moreover, coltsfoot contains antispasmodic compounds that can help relax the muscles in the respiratory tract, preventing the cough reflex. Lastly, coltsfoot tea has been traditionally used as a natural remedy for respiratory conditions, including bronchitis, asthma, and coughs, due to its soothing, expectorant, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of coltsfoot are often used in medicinal teas for coughs due to their mucilaginous properties, which can help soothe irritated throats and suppress coughing.
Flowers: The flowers of coltsfoot contain a substance called mucilage, which can help ease irritation in the respiratory tract, making it useful for treating coughs and other respiratory ailments.
Rhyzomes: The rhyzomes of coltsfoot contain various compounds with anti-tussive and expectorant properties, making them a popular choice for treating coughs and other respiratory conditions.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: Coltsfoot contains mucilage, which helps soothe irritated mucous membranes, providing relief from a persistent cough.
Zinc: This mineral, found in coltsfoot, can help reduce the severity and duration of coughs, as it has antitussive properties.
Selenium: Selenium, present in coltsfoot, helps support the immune system, which can help the body combat respiratory infections that lead to coughing.
Vitamin C: Coltsfoot contains Vitamin C, which can help alleviate coughs by supporting the immune system and acting as an expectorant.
Silica: Silica, found in coltsfoot, helps reduce inflammation, making it helpful in treating coughs caused by irritation or inflammation in the throat or airways.
remedy_recipe: Harvest: Pick 2-3 Coltsfoot leaves, ensuring they are from a clean and pesticide-free environment. This step can be done in early spring or late fall.
Rinse: Gently rinse the leaves under cold running water to remove any debris or dirt, then let them air dry. This should take only a few minutes.
Crush: Crush the dried leaves using a mortar and pestle or your hands to release their beneficial compounds. Allocate around 5-10 minutes for this process.
Brew: Place the crushed leaves in a teapot and pour 2 cups of boiling water over them. Allow the tea to steep for about 10-15 minutes.
Strain: Strain the tea to remove the leaves, pour it into a cup and consume. Discard the spent leaves. Enjoy your homemade coltsfoot tea for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Elecampane tea helps with cough because it contains compounds called alantolactone and isoalantolactone, which have been shown to have expectorant and anti-tussive properties, helping to expel mucus from the respiratory tract and alleviate coughing. Additionally, elecampane has anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce irritation in the throat and airways, further soothing coughs. The tea also has a warming effect on the body, which can help to stimulate circulation and promote the expulsion of mucus. Furthermore, elecampane has been used traditionally as a relaxant, which can help to alleviate tension and spasms in the respiratory muscles, reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Finally, elecampane tea is caffeine-free and has a mild, sweet flavor, making it a gentle and soothing option for people of all ages who are experiencing coughs.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of elecampane have expectorant properties, making it easier to cough up phlegm.
Rhizomes: Elecampane's rhizomes contain beneficial compounds that soothe irritated throats and reduce coughing.
Flowers: Elecampane flowers have mild sedative properties, helping to calm coughs and promote sleep.
Bark: The bark of elecampane contains compounds that help relax bronchial muscles, making it easier to breathe.
remedy_constituents: Isoalantolactone: This compound found in elecampane has been shown to have expectorant and anti-tussive properties, helping to loosen mucus and alleviate coughs.
Inulin: This type of soluble fiber in elecampane can help soothe irritated membranes and reduce coughing by providing a coating effect.

Helenalin: This sesquiterpene lactone in elecampane has been found to have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, thus alleviating cough.
Flavonoids: Found in elecampane, these compounds have antioxidant properties and can help protect lung tissue from damage and reduce inflammation, consequently helping to alleviate cough.
remedy_recipe: Chop: Chop 1-2 tablespoons of dried elecampane root into small pieces.
Steep: Place the chopped elecampane root in a tea infuser and steep in boiling water for 10-15 minutes.
Serve: Pour the tea into a cup and enjoy it while it's still warm, up to three times a day.

remedies.remedy_desc: Pleurisy tea helps with cough because it contains compounds with medicinal properties, such as dried leaves of pulmonaria and thyme, which have expectorant and soothing effects on the respiratory tract. These ingredients help to loosen and expel mucus from the lungs, making it easier to cough up and eliminating the irritants that cause the cough. Additionally, the natural oils found in pleurisy tea, such as eucalyptus and mint, have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties that can help reduce inflammation in the airways and suppress the cough reflex. Furthermore, the hot liquid of the tea can help to soothe a sore throat and alleviate irritation, providing relief and reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Finally, pleurisy tea has been used traditionally for its calming and sedative effects, which can help to promote rest and relaxation, allowing the body to recover from respiratory illnesses and reducing the need for coughing.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the pleurisy plant are used to make medicinal tea for cough due to their high content of therapeutic compounds that help soothe irritated throats and alleviate coughing.
Flowers: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory properties, the flowers of the pleurisy plant are used in tea to provide relief from cough and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Roots: The roots of the pleurisy plant are used to make medicinal tea for cough as they contain active compounds that help expel mucus from the lungs and suppress cough.
Bark: The bark of the pleurisy plant is used in teas to treat coughs because of its exceptional expectorant properties, which aid in the removal of phlegm from the respiratory system.
remedy_constituents: Expectorant: Compounds like 'selenium' and 'coumarins' in pleurisy root can help loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it beneficial for wet, productive coughs.
Antitussive: The alkaloid 'spirososide' found in pleurisy has a calming effect on the cough reflex, making it useful for dry, persistent coughs.
Analgesic: Compounds like 'rutin' in pleurisy root have anti-inflammatory properties that can help relieve pain and discomfort in the chest, often associated with a severe cough.
Anti-inflammatory: 'Quercetin' and other flavonoids in pleurisy root can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, providing relief from cough-inducing irritations.
Relaxant: Certain compounds in pleurisy, like 'kaempferol,' can help relax smooth muscles, easing constriction in the trachea and bronchi and alleviating cough symptoms.
remedy_recipe: In a saucepan, combine 1 teaspoon of dried pleurisy root, 1 tablespoon of dried licorice root, and 2 cups of water; bring to a boil.
Remove from heat and add 1 teaspoon of dried thyme leaves; let steep for 10 minutes.
Strain the tea, then stir in honey and lemon juice to taste.
Drink warm, up to three times daily, as needed for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Grindelia tea helps with cough because it contains antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties that soothe the respiratory tract, reducing irritation and inflammation. The tea's expectorant qualities help break up mucus, making it easier to expel from the lungs and alleviating congestion. Furthermore, Grindelia tea has been found to have a calming effect on cough reflexes, reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Additionally, it may help with asthma-related coughs by relaxing bronchial muscles, thus improving breathing. The overall soothing and healing effects of Grindelia tea on the respiratory system make it a popular natural remedy for treating coughs and related symptoms.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of Grindelia are used in medicinal tea for cough due to their anti-tussive and expectorant properties.
Flowers: Grindelia flowers are included in tea blends for cough relief as they have been found to have soothing and anti-inflammatory effects on respiratory tissues.
Roots: Grindelia roots are used in teas for cough treatment because of their saponin content, which helps to loosen phlegm and soothe irritated throats.
Seeds: The seeds of Grindelia contain essential oils, which are beneficial in medicinal teas for cough as they help to relax bronchial spasms and ease breathing.
remedy_constituents: Prenylated bisabolangeloids: These compounds found in grindelia have been shown to have anti-tumour, anti-inflammatory, and antispasmodic properties, helping to relax the airways and reduce coughing.
Sesquiterpene lactones: These compounds have been found to have expectorant, mucolytic, and bronchodilatory effects, aiding in the expulsion of mucus and reducing coughing.
Flavonoids: These antioxidant compounds have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, helping to soothe irritated airways and reduce coughing.
Polyynes: These compounds have been found to have expectorant and antimicrobial properties, helping to clear mucus and fight infections that can cause coughing.
Triterpenes: These compounds have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and expectorant effects, aiding in the expulsion of mucus and reducing coughing.
remedy_recipe: Heat 1 cup of water in a tea kettle or pot until boiling.
Add 1-2 teaspoons of dried grindelia herb to a tea infuser or teapot.
Pour the hot water over the herb and let it steep for 10-15 minutes.
Remove the tea infuser or strain the tea into a cup.
Drink the tea up to three times a day as needed for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Hyssop tea helps with cough because it has expectorant properties that aid in thinning and expelling mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to cough up and eliminate. The tea's anti-spasmodic and anti-inflammatory components also soothe irritated throat tissue, reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Furthermore, hyssop tea contains antiviral and antibacterial properties that can help combat the underlying causes of cough, such as infections. Additionally, it has been found to relax bronchial muscles, thus alleviating constriction and making breathing easier. Lastly, hyssop tea's calming effect on the nervous system can help reduce stress and promote better sleep, which is crucial for recovery from cough and respiratory ailments.
remedy_parts: Leaves: High in essential oils and flavonoids, leaves are commonly used in teas to soothe throat irritation and help alleviate cough.
Flowers: Rich in antioxidants, hyssop flowers can help reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms of cold and cough.
Stems: Containing rosmarinic acid, stems can help decrease mucus production and calm persistent coughing.
Seeds: Packed with nutrients and antioxidants, seeds can provide relief from respiratory ailments and reduce coughing.
remedy_constituents: Pinocamphones: These compounds found in hyssop act as expectorants, helping to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, thus alleviating cough.
Isopinocamphone: This compound also has expectorant properties, making it helpful in the treatment of wet coughs by clearing phlegm from the airways.

Flavonoids: Hyssop contains various flavonoids, such as luteolin and diosmin, which have anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic effects, helping to soothe cough and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Tannins: The tannins present in hyssop have astringent properties, helping to tighten and dry mucous membranes, which can provide relief from excessive coughing.
remedy_recipe: Heat one cup of water to boiling in a teapot or kettle, which should take about 3-5 minutes.
Crush 1-2 teaspoons of dried hyssop leaves, or 1 hyssop tea bag, to release the oils and improve the tea's potency.
Transfer the hot water to a teacup and add the crushed hyssop leaves or tea bag.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes to extract the herb's beneficial compounds.
Remove the hyssop leaves or tea bag and enjoy your soothing hyssop tea, preferably before bedtime.

remedies.remedy_desc: Fenugreek tea helps with cough because it contains anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties that soothe the throat, reduce inflammation, and help expel mucus. The tea's warm temperature also helps to alleviate congestion by dilating the bronchial tubes, making it easier to breathe. Additionally, fenugreek tea is rich in mucilage, a type of carbohydrate that forms a gel-like coating when it comes into contact with water. This coating can provide a protective layer to the mucous membranes in the throat and lungs, further reducing irritation and coughing. Moreover, fenugreek tea has been shown to have antimicrobial and antiviral properties, which may help to fight off respiratory infections that can cause coughing fits. Finally, fenugreek tea is a natural and caffeine-free beverage, making it a safer and healthier alternative to over-the-counter cough suppressants, which often contain harsh chemicals and additives.
remedy_parts: Seeds: Fenugreek seeds are commonly used due to their expectorant properties, helping to clear mucus and soothe coughs.
Leaves: The leaves of the fenugreek plant are often used in tea due to their mucilage content, which can help to relieve irritation in the throat and reduce coughing.
Flowers: Fenugreek flowers contain anti-inflammatory compounds that can help to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviate coughing.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: Fenugreek contains a significant amount of mucilage, a substance that forms a soothing film over the mucous membranes, providing relief from irritation and inflammation in the respiratory tract, thus alleviating cough.
Saponins: Fenugreek contains saponins, which have expectorant properties, helping to loosen and expel mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel phlegm and reducing cough.
Diosgenin: This compound found in fenugreek has anti-inflammatory and antitussive properties, helping to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and relieve cough.
Vitamin C: Fenugreek contains high levels of vitamin C, an antioxidant that helps boost the immune system and reduce inflammation, providing relief from symptoms of cough and cold.

Steroidal saponins: These saponins, such as yamogenin and gitogenin, found in fenugreek, have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, helping to alleviate cough by reducing inflammation in the respiratory tract and aiding in the expulsion of mucus.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 teaspoon of dried fenugreek seeds; bring to a boil, then reduce heat and simmer for 10 minutes.
Turn off heat, add 1 teaspoon of honey and 1/2 teaspoon of lemon juice, then stir well.
Cover pot and let the mixture steep for 5 minutes.
Drink warm, up to three times a day as needed for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Chamomile tea helps with cough because it contains several compounds with anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and soothing properties. The most notable of these compounds are chamazulene and bisabolol, which can help reduce inflammation in the airways and alleviate coughing. Chamomile tea also acts as a natural expectorant, helping to thin and expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Furthermore, its mild sedative effects can promote relaxation and facilitate a better night's sleep, which is crucial for recovery from respiratory ailments. Lastly, chamomile tea's soothing warmth can help alleviate irritation in the throat and provide immediate relief to a persistent cough.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Chamomile flowers are commonly used in medicinal tea for cough due to their natural anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties.
Leaves: The leaves of chamomile plants are often included in tea blends to soothe irritation in the throat and help relieve coughing.
Seeds: Chamomile seeds contain essential oils that have calming effects on the body, making them useful for reducing coughing fits and promoting relaxation.
remedy_constituents: Apigenin: This flavonoid has antitussive and smooth muscle relaxant properties, helping to reduce coughing and spasms in the respiratory tract.
Chamazulene: This aromatic chemical has anti-inflammatory effects, which can help alleviate irritation and swelling in the airways, reducing coughs caused by inflammation.
Bisabolol: A sesquiterpene alcohol, bisabolol has anti-inflammatory and spasmolytic properties, helping to soothe coughs and relax the muscles in the respiratory tract.

Matricin: This chemical is converted to chamazulene during steam distillation and has anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties, which can help relieve cough and inflammation in the respiratory tract.
Higenamine: An alkaloid, higenamine acts as a bronchodilator and expectorant, helping to clear mucus and expand the airways, reducing coughs and making breathing easier.
remedy_recipe: Boil 1 cup of water in a tea kettle or saucepan for 3-5 minutes.
Add 1 heaping teaspoon of dried chamomile flowers to a tea infuser or strainer in a cup.
Pour the hot water over the chamomile flowers and let it steep for 5-7 minutes.
Stir in 1 teaspoon of honey (optional) for added soothing effects.
Enjoy the warm chamomile tea 2-3 times a day for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Yerba santa tea helps with cough because it contains antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory compounds, such as taraxerol and oleanolic acid, which soothe irritated mucous membranes and reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract. This herbal tea has been traditionally used to alleviate symptoms of upper respiratory infections, including coughs, by promoting expectoration and clearing phlegm from the airways. Additionally, yerba santa tea may help to relieve irritants in the throat, thereby reducing the frequency and intensity of coughing fits. Furthermore, its analgesic properties can help to numb the throat, offering temporary relief from cough-related discomfort and pain. Lastly, yerba santa tea has been found to have antimicrobial and antiviral effects, potentially helping to combat the underlying causes of coughs and support overall respiratory health.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Yerba Santa leaves are commonly used as they contain phenolic acids and flavonoids which help soothe coughs and reduce inflammation.
Stems: The stems of Yerba Santa have essential oils and tannins that provide astringent properties, helpful in treating persistent coughs.
Flowers: Yerba Santa flowers are occasionally used in teas, as they contain antioxidants and analgesic properties, providing relief from cough and irritation.
remedy_constituents: Eriodictyol: This flavonoid has been found to have anti-tumor, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties. It may help reduce cough and irritation in the respiratory system.
Luteolin: This flavonoid has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. It can help soothe inflammation in the respiratory system, thereby alleviating cough.
Linarin: This flavonoid has been found to have antitussive and expectorant properties. It can help reduce cough and loosen phlegm.
Beta-sitosterol: This plant sterol has anti-inflammatory properties. It can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory system, thereby alleviating cough.
Stigmasterol: This plant sterol has anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties. It can help reduce inflammation and loosen phlegm in the respiratory system, thereby alleviating cough.
remedy_recipe: Harvest or obtain 2-3 sprigs of fresh Yerba Santa leaves, rinse them gently and set aside.
Bring 4 cups of water to a boil, then add the Yerba Santa leaves and reduce heat to a simmer.
Allow the tea to simmer for 15-20 minutes to extract the medicinal properties.
Strain the tea to remove leaves, then let it cool down to a drinkable temperature.
Sweeten with honey if desired, and drink 3 cups a day, between meals, to soothe cough and irritation.

remedies.remedy_desc: Horehound tea helps with cough because it contains marrubium, a compound that stimulates the flow of saliva and mucus, which can relieve congestion and soothe throat irritation. Furthermore, horehound has been found to have expectorant properties, which means it can help expel mucus from the respiratory tract. Its anti-tussive and anti-inflammatory effects also help to suppress coughing fits, making it a popular natural remedy for respiratory ailments such as bronchitis, laryngitis, and asthma. Horehound tea is generally considered safe and has been used as a traditional medicine for centuries, but it is always best to consult with a healthcare provider before adding any new supplements to your routine. Additionally, pregnant or breastfeeding women and people with pre-existing medical conditions should exercise caution and seek medical advice before consuming horehound tea.
remedy_parts: Leaves: High in antioxidants and contain Marrubiin, which helps to reduce inflammation and soothe a cough.
Flowers: Contain essential oils and antioxidants, which help to alleviate symptoms of cough and respiratory issues.
Stems: Have anti-tussive properties, meaning they help to reduce coughing by relaxing the throat muscles.
Roots: Contain active compounds that are expectorants and help to break up mucus and provide relief from a persistent cough.
remedy_constituents: Marrubiin: This compound found in horehound has been shown to help loosen phlegm and promote expectoration, making it useful for treating wet coughs.
Pulegone: This constituent has antispasmodic and relaxant properties, helping to relieve persistent coughing and spasms in the respiratory tract.
Flavonoids: These antioxidants have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help soothe irritated throat tissue and alleviate coughing.
Tannins: The astringent tannins in horehound can help reduce inflammation and soothe mucous membranes, providing relief for cough and irritated throats.

Volatile oils: The volatile oils in horehound, including menthol-like compounds, can help to expectorate phlegm, reduce inflammation and provide a soothing effect on the throat, thus alleviating cough.
remedy_recipe: Crush 1-2 tablespoons of dried horehound leaves to release their oils, and place them in a teapot.
Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the crushed leaves, and let the tea steep for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea to remove the leaves, and discard them.
Drink the tea while warm, up to three times a day as needed for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Plantain tea helps with cough because it contains mucilage, a substance that becomes a slippery gel when mixed with water. This gel can soothe and protect inflamed mucous membranes, reducing irritation in the throat and suppressing coughs. Plantain tea also has anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, which help to reduce inflammation in the airways and expel excess mucus. Additionally, it contains antioxidants that can help to reduce oxidative stress and support overall respiratory health. The leaves of the plantain plant are also known to have antibacterial and antiviral properties, which may help to fight off infections that can cause a cough. Finally, plantain tea is a natural and caffeine-free alternative to over-the-counter cough medicines, making it a safe and effective option for people of all ages.
remedy_parts: Leaves - The leaves of the plantain plant are used due to their mucilaginous properties that help soothe irritated throats and reduce coughing.
Seeds - Plantain seeds are rich in mucilage and have expectorant and demulcent properties, making them useful in treating coughs and respiratory conditions.
Bark - The bark of the plantain plant contains beneficial compounds that have been used to treat coughs and other respiratory ailments.
remedy_constituents: Mucilage: This constituent forms a soothing film over inflamed mucous membranes, relieving irritation and reducing coughing.
Irritant compounds: Plantain contains compounds that can cause irritation when inhaled, helping to stimulate coughing and clear mucus from the airways.
Tannins: These astringent compounds help to reduce inflammation and soothe irritated tissues, providing relief from coughing.

Flavonoids: Certain flavonoids found in plantain, such as luteolin and apigenin, have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and expectorant effects, helping to relieve coughs and clear mucus from the respiratory tract.
Silica: Plantain is high in silica, a mineral that helps to promote tissue repair, reduce inflammation, and support overall lung health.
Sterols: Sterols found in plantain, such as beta-sitosterol, have anti-inflammatory and immune-boosting effects, helping to reduce coughs and support respiratory health.
remedy_recipe: Peel and chop 2 ripe plantains into small pieces, then rinse them under cold water.
In a pot, combine plantains with 4 cups of water and bring the mixture to a boil.
Reduce heat and let it simmer for 15-20 minutes, or until the plantains are soft.
Strain the liquid to remove plantain pieces, then let it cool down for a few minutes.
Pour the plantain tea into a cup and drink it while warm, up to three times a day as needed.

remedies.remedy_desc: Sage tea helps with cough because it contains rosmarinic acid and other volatile compounds, such as thujone, camphor, and eucalyptus, which have anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties. These components help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, soothe coughs, and ease congestion by promoting expectoration. Moreover, sage tea's antimicrobial properties can help fight off respiratory infections that might cause a cough. Additionally, its soothing warmth can help alleviate throat irritation and provide comfort. Lastly, sage tea is a natural alternative to over-the-counter cough remedies, with fewer side effects and added health benefits.
remedy_parts: Leaves: The leaves of the sage plant contain essential oils and have anti-spasmodic properties, making them effective in soothing coughs.
Flowers: Sage flowers are rich in flavonoids and volatile oils, which can help reduce inflammation and relieve symptoms of coughs and colds.
Stems: The stems of the sage plant contain rosmarinic acid, which has been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, making it useful for treating respiratory ailments.
Buds: Sage buds are a concentrated source of the plant's active compounds, including camphor and thujone, which can help break up mucus and relieve congestion.
Roots: The roots of the sage plant contain a high concentration of antioxidants and have expectorant properties, making them useful for treating persistent coughs and respiratory infections.
remedy_constituents: Essential oil: The essential oil of sage contains compounds such as eucalyptol and thujone, which have expectorant and cough suppressant properties, helping to alleviate coughing.
Tannins: Tannins in sage have astringent properties, helping to soothe inflamed mucous membranes and reducing coughing.
Flavonoids: Flavonoids like diosmetin and luteolin have anti-inflammatory properties, helping to reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract and alleviate cough.
Terpenes: Terpenes such as α- and β-pinene and camphene have expectorant and bronchodilatory effects, aiding in the expulsion of mucus and improving breathing, thereby reducing cough.

Phenolic acids: Phenolic acids like rosmarinic and caffeic acid have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, protecting lung tissue from oxidative stress and inflammation, further alleviating cough.
remedy_recipe: Pick 2-3 fresh sage leaves from your garden or purchase a small bunch from a local market.
Rinse the sage leaves under cool running water, then pat dry with a clean towel.
Coarsely chop the sage leaves, then place them in a teapot or heat-resistant cup.
Heat 8 ounces of water just to boiling, then pour it over the chopped sage leaves.
Allow the tea to steep for 5-10 minutes, then strain and enjoy up to 3 times daily as needed for cough relief.

remedies.remedy_desc: Rosemary tea helps with cough because it contains rosmarinus officinalis, an ingredient with anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and expectorant properties that help soothe a cough and thin mucus. The tea's warm temperature also helps to alleviate congestion by loosening up phlegm and making it easier to expel. Furthermore, rosemary tea's antioxidants assist in strengthening the immune system, which aids in fighting off infections that can cause a cough. Additionally, rosemary tea's calming effects can help reduce coughing fits and promote better sleep, which is crucial for recovery. Lastly, rosemary tea's soothing aroma can help open up the airways and alleviate breathing difficulties, providing relief for individuals suffering from a cough.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Rosemary leaves are rich in aromatic compounds and rosmarinic acid, which have anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, making them effective at soothing coughs and clearing respiratory passages.
Flowers: The fragrant rosemary flowers contain essential oils and antioxidants that help relax the throat and alleviate irritation, providing relief from persistent coughs.
Stems: Though less potent than the leaves, rosemary stems still contain helpful compounds that can ease coughing and soothe inflamed tissues in the respiratory tract.
remedy_constituents: Rosmarinic acid: This compound has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce inflammation in the respiratory tract, relieving cough and related symptoms.
Caffeic acid: Acting as an expectorant, caffeic acid can help thin and clear mucus in the airways, making it easier to expel and alleviating cough.
Carnosic acid: With its antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, carnosic acid can protect lung tissues from damage, supporting cough relief and respiratory health.

Essential oils: The essential oils in rosemary, including α-pinene and 1,8-cineole, have expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties that can help loosen phlegm and soothe irritated airways, thereby alleviating cough.
remedy_recipe: In a pot, combine 1 cup of water and 1 sprig of fresh rosemary, and bring to a boil, which may take around 5 minutes.
Reduce heat to low, cover, and let it simmer for approximately 10 minutes to allow the rosemary to release its medicinal properties.
Turn off the heat, then add 1 teaspoon of honey and 1 slice of lemon for added soothing and vitamin C benefits, stirring until the honey is fully dissolved.
Strain the tea to remove the rosemary sprig and any solid particles, then let it cool down for 2-3 minutes before drinking.
Sip the warm rosemary tea slowly to soothe a cough and relieve congestion, ideally before bedtime.

remedies.remedy_desc: Basil tea helps with cough because it has anti-inflammatory and expectorant properties, which help to reduce inflammation in the airways and promote the expulsion of mucus. The presence of caffeic acid and eugenol in basil tea also aids in providing relief from a cough by acting as natural decongestants and soothing throats. Additionally, basil tea has antispasmodic effects that can help relax the muscles of the respiratory tract, reducing coughing fits. Furthermore, basil tea can boost the immune system, helping the body fight off infections that may cause coughs. Lastly, basil tea's warm temperature can help soothe irritated throat tissues, providing instant relief and alleviating symptoms of a cough.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Basil leaves are commonly used in medicinal teas for cough due to their expectorant properties, helping to expel mucus from the respiratory tract.
Flowers: The fragrant basil flowers contain essential oils and antioxidants, making them suitable for soothing throat irritations and alleviating cough symptoms.
Seeds: Basil seeds are mucilaginous, providing a demulcent effect in tea, which can help soothe inflammation and reduce coughing fits.
remedy_constituents: Eugenol: This compound in basil has antitussive and expectorant properties, helping to alleviate coughs by reducing irritation in the respiratory tract and expelling mucus.
Rosmarinic Acid: With its anti-inflammatory properties, rosmarinic acid can help soothe irritated airways, providing relief from coughing spasms.
Ocimene: This monoterpene in basil has been shown to have antitussive effects, helping to suppress coughs and ease breathing.
Linalool: Linalool, a terpene alcohol found in basil, has been found to have antitussive properties, helping to reduce coughs by inhibiting cough reflexes.
β-Caryophyllene: This sesquiterpene in basil has anti-inflammatory and analgesic effects, which can help alleviate cough-related discomfort and inflammation.
remedy_recipe: Boil 2 cups of water in a saucepan for 3-5 minutes.
Add 2 tablespoons of fresh basil leaves to the boiling water.
Strain the tea to remove basil leaves, then add honey or lemon juice to taste.
Drink warm, up to three times a day for cough relief.
